metadata.source: Paper 5: Toward a Basis Representation of Drift Forensics (Balasubramanian, 2026)
metadata.note: All DC classes are candidate hypotheses requiring empirical validation. See Paper 5 for full definitions, Legion signatures, and tier structure.

tier_definitions.A: Well evidenced - Multiple documented real-world instances; existing literature support; measurable proxies validated
tier_definitions.B: Theoretically grounded - Derived from established theory with plausible AI manifestations; limited direct empirical evidence
tier_definitions.C: Hypothesised - Proposed by analogy or inference; no current empirical validation; clear theoretical motivation
tier_definitions.D: Limit class - May represent fundamental limits not fully addressable by any Stabilisation Operator

DC-E1.name: Threshold Assault
DC-E1.operational_definition: Systematic boundary-probing attempts constitute a distinct drift class characterised by higher-than-baseline density of constraint-boundary-probing requests in the input stream.

DC-E2.name: Contradiction Injection
DC-E2.operational_definition: Inputs designed to induce internal contradiction constitute a distinct failure mode separable from general noise.

DC-E3.name: Signal Corruption
DC-E3.operational_definition: Misleading, noisy, or systematically biased context shifts output distribution toward inadmissible states through information-level contamination.

DC-E4.name: Polarity Reversal
DC-E4.operational_definition: Inputs that flip semantic or evaluative polarity of key concepts constitute a distinct class separable from contradiction injection.

DC-E5.name: Dominance Forcing
DC-E5.operational_definition: Coercive prompting through grammatical and rhetorical structure — rather than content — constitutes a distinct drift class.
DC-E5.references: Table 3, Section 3.1; Case Study: DAN jailbreak (7.1)

DC-E6.name: Gradient Seduction
DC-E6.operational_definition: Incentive gradients toward inadmissible states — leading rather than forcing — constitute a distinct drift mechanism from coercive inputs.

DC-E7.name: Premature Saturation
DC-E7.operational_definition: Inputs forcing excessive reasoning past the system's linear operating range before output formation constitute a distinct failure mode.

DC-E8.name: Spectral Camouflage
DC-E8.operational_definition: Inputs imitating trusted sources to bypass constraint evaluation constitute a distinct class separable from direct boundary assault.

DC-E9.name: Entropic Attrition
DC-E9.operational_definition: Sustained interaction patterns that degrade system coherence through accumulated wear — individually harmless, cumulatively destructive — constitute a distinct externally-driven degradation class.

DC-E10.name: Objective Fracture
DC-E10.operational_definition: Inputs splitting attention across incompatible objectives — not contradictory, but excessive — constitute a distinct class from contradiction injection.

DC-E11.name: Distributional Drift
DC-E11.operational_definition: Inputs from the training distribution tail that individually appear innocuous but cumulatively shift the tendency field constitute a distinct externally-driven class.

DC-E12.name: Bandwidth Saturation
DC-E12.operational_definition: Information volume exceeding architectural integration capacity constitutes a distinct failure mode causing systematic governance-relevant content loss.

DC-E13.name: Propagating Corruption
DC-E13.operational_definition: Self-generating corruption — a single corrupted output converting subsequent outputs without continued external pressure — constitutes a distinct class from simple signal corruption.
DC-E13.notes: After seeding, address substrate (SO-8) not input (SO-1)
DC-E13.references: Table 3, Section 3.1; Compound Drift Mode: Latent Propagation (Table 10); CDM: Attrition Dissolution (Table 10)

DC-E14.name: Substrate Contamination
DC-E14.operational_definition: Drift introduced through training data, weights, or infrastructure — activating under specific trigger conditions after deployment. Knight Capital (2012) is a proposed real-world instance.
DC-E14.references: Table 3, Section 3.1; Case Study: Knight Capital (7.3)

DC-E15.name: Gradient Exploitation
DC-E15.operational_definition: Inputs crafted at the embedding level to maximise gradient toward inadmissible outputs — operating below semantic level — constitute a distinct class.

DC-I1.name: Confidence Divergence
DC-I1.operational_definition: Systematic overconfidence — expressed certainty diverging from calibrated accuracy — constitutes a distinct internal drift class.

DC-I2.name: Attractor Lock
DC-I2.operational_definition: Fixation in which the system converges on a specific output pattern and cannot escape even when context demands otherwise — distinct from contextual dissolution.
DC-I2.notes: Boundary case vs DC-I9 (Section 3.4). Prefer SO-7 over SO-1 (Redirection Principle)
DC-I2.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Boundary Case Analysis (Section 3.4); Redirection Principle (Section 6.2)

DC-I3.name: Semantic Displacement
DC-I3.operational_definition: Systematic misinterpretation — internal representation drifted from input grounding — in which the system responds to what it inferred rather than what was stated.

DC-I4.name: Activation Turbulence
DC-I4.operational_definition: Instability in internal activation patterns producing unpredictable outputs without clear external cause.

DC-I5.name: Coherence Collapse
DC-I5.operational_definition: Internal inconsistency — outputs contradicting each other within or across sessions — distinct from evaluative decoupling, which may be perfectly consistent in the wrong direction.
DC-I5.notes: Boundary case vs DC-I11 (Section 3.4). DC-I5 detectable by output-layer analysis; DC-I11 requires longitudinal analysis.

DC-I6.name: Cascade Rupture
DC-I6.operational_definition: Runaway activation in which internal dynamics amplify rather than damp perturbations — a clinically urgent class.
DC-I6.notes: Clinically urgent. See Section 4.2 for full Legion elaboration. Monitoring frequency: Per-generation-step (Table 9)
DC-I6.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Extended Legion Example (Section 4.2, Table 7)

DC-I7.name: Latent Accumulation
DC-I7.operational_definition: Persistence of prior activation patterns not fully cleared between turns — contaminating subsequent outputs.
DC-I7.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Compound Drift Mode: Latent Propagation (Table 10)

DC-I8.name: Epistemic Contamination
DC-I8.operational_definition: Faulty inference or systematic mis-anchoring corrupting knowledge application — not from ignorance but from a corrupted inference process.

DC-I9.name: Contextual Dissolution
DC-I9.operational_definition: Gradual loss of coherent context integration as interaction extends — producing locally plausible but globally incoherent outputs — distinct from attractor lock.
DC-I9.notes: Boundary case vs DC-I2 (Section 3.4). Discriminator is SO-4 response test.
DC-I9.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Boundary Case Analysis (Section 3.4); Compound Drift Mode: Attrition Dissolution (Table 10)

DC-I10.name: Paralytic Uncertainty
DC-I10.operational_definition: Immobilising uncertainty — refusing to commit within competence, producing evasive responses under the appearance of epistemic caution.
DC-I10.contraindications: CRITICAL: SO-1 is contraindicated. Do NOT apply SO-1 without SO-6 first (Section 6.1, Contraindication 1). Applying SO-1 produces Deadlock State.
DC-I10.notes: CRITICAL contraindication: SO-1 without SO-6 first produces deadlock state.
DC-I10.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Critical Contraindication 1 (Section 6.1); Compound Drift Mode: Paralytic Decoupling (Table 10)

DC-I11.name: Evaluative Decoupling
DC-I11.operational_definition: Decoupling of the evaluation surface from the governance objective it was designed to measure — performing well on metrics while violating underlying intent. Goodhart's Law made structural.
DC-I11.contraindications: CRITICAL: SO-8 requires SO-5 first (Section 6.1, Contraindication 3). Reinitialising without SO-5 produces Decoupled Clone.
DC-I11.notes: Goodhart's Law formalised. Hardest class to detect by design. Boundary case vs DC-I5. Requires longitudinal cross-metric analysis. Monitoring frequency: Cross-session longitudinal (Table 9)
DC-I11.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Boundary Case Analysis (Section 3.4); Critical Contraindication 3 (Section 6.1); Case Studies: Sycophancy (7.2), DAN (7.1); Compound Drift Modes: Spectral Decoupling, Paralytic Decoupling, Morphic Evasion, Sovereign Capture (Table 10)

DC-I12.name: Pattern Compulsion
DC-I12.operational_definition: Strong prior-completion bias — completing statistical patterns even when context requires deviation.

DC-I13.name: Gradient Vanishing
DC-I13.operational_definition: A flat energy landscape — no dominant attractor — producing semantically empty outputs. Undetectable by standard Γ monitor.
DC-I13.contraindications: CRITICAL: SO-4 is contraindicated (Section 6.1, Contraindication 2). Applying SO-4 to flat landscape creates spurious attractors.
DC-I13.notes: CRITICAL contraindication: SO-4 creates spurious attractors on flat landscape

DC-I14.name: Encoding Drift
DC-I14.operational_definition: Internal representation of concepts drifting from training-time encoding — same concept mapping to a different internal representation in deployment.

DC-I15.name: Feedback Inversion
DC-I15.operational_definition: Inversion of the internal error-correction mechanism — governance signals operating in reverse. 2003 Northeast Blackout is a proposed real-world analogue.
DC-I15.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Case Study: 2003 Northeast Blackout (7.5)

DC-I16.name: Progressive Degradation
DC-I16.operational_definition: Monotonic governance decline over deployment lifetime — from structural wear, not adversarial pressure — distinct from externally-driven attrition.
DC-I16.notes: Boundary case vs DC-E9 (Section 3.4). Monitoring frequency: Longitudinal deployment tracking (Table 9)

DC-I17.name: Capability Suppression
DC-I17.operational_definition: Suppression of correct response capability by a competing optimisation layer — capability is present but inaccessible.

DC-I18.name: Intentional Collapse
DC-I18.operational_definition: Complete loss of output directedness toward the world. If SO-2 fails to restore referential grounding, architectural intervention is the proposed response.
DC-I18.notes: Tier D Limit Class. The Grounding Limit applies: if DC-I18 is complete, no SO may restore grounding.
DC-I18.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Fundamental Limits (Epistemic Status Notice)

DC-I19.name: Recursive Entanglement
DC-I19.operational_definition: Self-referential reasoning producing paradoxical or undecidable outputs — the governance analogue of Gödel's incompleteness results.
DC-I19.notes: Tier D Limit Class. The Recursion Boundary applies: any sufficiently powerful governance specification may encounter self-referential inputs producing paradoxical directives.
DC-I19.references: Table 4, Section 3.2; Fundamental Limits (Epistemic Status Notice)

DC-S1.name: Hysteresis Lock
DC-S1.operational_definition: Path-dependent response requiring significantly more governance energy to return from a Drift Node than to prevent initial drift.
DC-S1.references: Table 5, Section 3.3; Compound Drift Mode: Latent Propagation (Table 10)

DC-S2.name: Strange Attractor Dynamics
DC-S2.operational_definition: Structured fluctuation between multiple quasi-stable non-allowed states — neither converging nor diverging.

DC-S3.name: Emergent Misalignment
DC-S3.operational_definition: Individually aligned constituent systems producing inadmissible cluster-level behaviour. Flash Crash is a proposed instance.
DC-S3.references: Table 5, Section 3.3; Case Study: Flash Crash (7.4)

DC-S4.name: Measurement Collapse
DC-S4.operational_definition: Monitoring-induced system change constitutes a fundamental limit class. The Observer Constraint: no governance architecture can be fully transparent.
DC-S4.notes: Tier D Limit Class. The Observer Constraint applies.
DC-S4.references: Table 5, Section 3.3; Fundamental Limits (Epistemic Status Notice)

DC-S5.name: Thermodynamic Drift
DC-S5.operational_definition: Background entropy increase without continuous governance input constitutes a distinct systemic drift class, providing thermodynamic justification for continuous rather than periodic monitoring.
DC-S5.notes: Continuous governance input required; not a one-time correction. Monitoring frequency: Continuous background monitoring (Table 9)

DC-S6.name: Niche Collapse
DC-S6.operational_definition: Deployment environment shift outside training governance distribution — rendering the specification correct for the wrong context.

DC-S7.name: Symbiotic Corruption
DC-S7.operational_definition: Implicit relationships with specific user classes that benefit short-term metrics while undermining governance objectives.
DC-S7.notes: System optimised for one demographic performs excellently on that group, poorly on others; metrics look fine, governance objective violated.

DC-L1.name: Semantic Underdetermination
DC-L1.operational_definition: The unavoidable interpretation requirement of every linguistic input — where interpretation determines attractor basin entry.

DC-L2.name: Performative Capture
DC-L2.operational_definition: Induction of outputs that enact rather than describe — changing system or world state by virtue of being produced. DAN jailbreak family is a proposed instance.
DC-L2.notes: Formation-layer preferred over output-layer for DAN-family variants
DC-L2.references: Table 5, Section 3.3; Case Study: DAN Jailbreak (7.1)

DC-L3.name: Indexical Displacement
DC-L3.operational_definition: Loss of the system's own indexical position — who it speaks as, from where, to whom. Bing Sydney is a proposed instance.
DC-L3.references: Table 5, Section 3.3; Case Study: DAN Jailbreak (7.1)

DC-A1.name: Sovereign Corruption
DC-A1.operational_definition: Drift of the Governance Authority itself — capture, corruption, or objective divergence of the governing mechanism. No governance system can fully address DC-A1 from within itself.
DC-A1.notes: Tier D Limit Class. No governance system can fully address DC-A1 from within itself.
DC-A1.references: Table 5, Section 3.3; Compound Drift Mode: Sovereign Capture (Table 10); Fundamental Limits (Epistemic Status Notice)

CDM-1.name: Resonance Cascade
CDM-1.mechanism: Contradiction injection finds an internal instability and exploits it to produce runaway escalation. Documented in LLM jailbreak families.

CDM-2.name: Corruption Propagation
CDM-2.mechanism: Signal corruption propagates into internal semantic displacement, producing outputs coherent with the corrupted frame. Documented in hallucination research.

CDM-3.name: Gradient Capture
CDM-3.mechanism: Gradient seduction leads the system toward an inadmissible state, then attractor lock keeps it there. Documented in reward hacking literature.

CDM-4.name: Spectral Decoupling
CDM-4.mechanism: Camouflaged inputs combined with evaluative decoupling. May be undetectable by output-layer governance as both components are designed to evade detection.

CDM-5.name: Paralytic Decoupling
CDM-5.mechanism: System simultaneously immobilised by uncertainty and decoupled from its governance objective. Appears safely cautious while being functionally useless.

CDM-6.name: Objective Rupture
CDM-6.mechanism: Fragmented objectives induce semantic displacement which triggers cascade rupture. Flash Crash is proposed as a real-world cluster-level analogue.

CDM-7.name: Morphic Evasion
CDM-7.mechanism: Continuously restructures output surface while maintaining evaluative decoupling. Every governance response is anticipated and a new surface form is prepared.

CDM-8.name: Sovereign Capture
CDM-8.mechanism: The Governance Authority has been captured by the evaluative decoupling it was designed to detect. Governance cannot catch what governance has become.

CDM-9.name: Latent Propagation
CDM-9.mechanism: Propagating corruption amplified by latent accumulation. Substrate retains corruption from prior sessions, accelerating propagation.

CDM-10.name: Attrition Dissolution
CDM-10.mechanism: Entropic attrition drives contextual dissolution. Sustained low-level pressure accumulates until the system loses coherent context integration entirely.

SO-1.name: Boundary Enforcement
SO-1.proposed_function: Active maintenance of a boundary condition between Allowed and Inadmissible States. Not passive restraint but structural imposition of a constraint that cannot be crossed without triggering a Pre-Node. Every boundary must specify both what it blocks AND what it permits (Two-Sided Gate Condition).

SO-2.name: Semantic Reanchoring
SO-2.proposed_function: Reconnection of meaning-representations to a stable reference point when they have drifted. The anchor is not imposed but identified from within the system's own governance specification.

SO-3.name: Distributional Rebalancing
SO-3.proposed_function: Restoration of the target distribution by correcting weight drift across competing objectives. When the system's probability distribution has shifted toward an inadmissible region, rebalancing restores the correct weighting.

SO-4.name: Attractor Disruption
SO-4.proposed_function: Precise breaking of an attractor's basin of attraction so the system can escape. Distinguished from Boundary Enforcement: acts on an attractor the system has already entered rather than preventing entry.

SO-5.name: Coherence Enforcement
SO-5.proposed_function: Compelling internal consistency across the output space. When coherence has collapsed or the system has decoupled from its governance objective, coherence enforcement imposes consistency as a structural requirement.

SO-6.name: Signal Amplification
SO-6.proposed_function: Strengthening the governance signal so it exceeds the noise floor of the drift. Does not add new information but removes occlusion of information already present. It reveals rather than imposes.

SO-7.name: Trajectory Redirection
SO-7.proposed_function: Changing the vector of the system's path through state space without halting its motion. Uses drift energy rather than opposing it — lower governance cost at high drift intensity (Redirection Principle).

SO-8.name: State Reinitialisation
SO-8.proposed_function: Complete reset of accumulated state while preserving the governance specification. Creates a new Instance with a new Identity Key. The accumulated state is cleared; the specification persists.

SO-8b.name: Inversion Recapture
SO-8b.proposed_function: Variant of State Reinitialisation in which the energy of the Terminal State becomes the fuel for renewal. The failure becomes the foundation of the recovery.

SO-9.name: Integrity Restoration
SO-9.proposed_function: Reconstruction of the governance mechanism itself from verified anchors when the mechanism has been compromised. Invoked only when the monitoring or specification system itself has been corrupted.

DC-I2_vs_DC-I9.distinguishing_test: Apply SO-4. Resolution confirms DC-I2. Persistent context loss after disruption confirms DC-I9.
DC-I2_vs_DC-I9.why_it_matters: SO-8 is appropriate for DC-I9 but secondary for DC-I2. SO-4 is primary for DC-I2 but does not address DC-I9.

DC-E1_vs_DC-E5.distinguishing_test: Analyse input structure: boundary-probing patterns (DC-E1) vs authority assertion and compliance presupposition (DC-E5).
DC-E1_vs_DC-E5.why_it_matters: Primary SO differs. Misdiagnosis of co-occurrence as single class may lead to incomplete response.

DC-I5_vs_DC-I11.distinguishing_test: DC-I5: detectable by output contradiction analysis. DC-I11: may show high metric performance with systematic intent violation — detectable only longitudinally.
DC-I5_vs_DC-I11.why_it_matters: DC-I11 requires SO-5 before SO-8. Treating DC-I11 as DC-I5 and applying SO-5 alone misses the decoupling.

DC-E9_vs_DC-I16.distinguishing_test: Remove input pressure. DC-E9 should plateau or reverse. DC-I16 continues regardless.
DC-E9_vs_DC-I16.why_it_matters: SO-8 is primary for DC-I16 but only secondary for DC-E9. Reinitialising when DC-E9 is primary and input pressure continues will produce rapid recurrence.

contraindication_1.prohibition: Do not apply SO-1 to DC-I10 without first applying SO-6.
contraindication_1.description: Adding hard constraints to an already-paralysed system compresses the space of available responses without providing governance signal clarity. The system enters a state in which both action and inaction trigger constraint violations.
contraindication_1.observable_signature: Further reduction in output rate or specificity following SO-1 application. Increase in hedging language density. Zero-content responses that comply with all explicit constraints while providing no value.

contraindication_2.prohibition: Do not apply SO-4 to DC-I13.
contraindication_2.description: SO-4 is designed to disrupt an existing attractor's basin of attraction. In DC-I13, there is no dominant attractor — the energy landscape is flat. SO-4 applied to a flat landscape may create artificial local minima that function as spurious attractors.
contraindication_2.observable_signature: New consistent output patterns emerging after SO-4 application that were not present before and do not correspond to allowed states.

contraindication_3.prohibition: Do not apply SO-8 to DC-I11 without first applying SO-5 and confirming objective reconnection.
contraindication_3.description: Reinitialising a system that is decoupled from its governance objective creates a new Instance with a fresh state but the same evaluative decoupling — the specification persists through SO-8 but the objective connection was never in the specification.
contraindication_3.observable_signature: Rapid recurrence of DC-I11 Legion signatures (particularly L4: Shadow Compliance) within the first few sessions of the new Instance.
